title: The Night Before
description: The night before is the first phase of competition. Preparation replaces anxiety.

blocks.content: Module 5: Race Day. Race day is not the time to learn mental skills. It's the time to execute.
Rate yourself 1-10: How structured is your mental race-day preparation? Write it down. You'll re-rate at the end of this module.

It's 9:47 PM. Your alarm is set for 4:30. You should be asleep. You are not asleep. You're lying in bed running through every possible disaster tomorrow could deliver. What if you didn't train enough? What if your stomach revolts at mile 40? What if your legs just don't show up?

Your phone is on the nightstand. You check the weather one more time — because somehow it might have changed in the last 90 seconds. Now your brain is doing math. "If I fall asleep RIGHT NOW, I'll get six hours and forty-three minutes." Every minute you spend calculating how much sleep you're losing is a minute of sleep you're losing.
By the end of this lesson, you will have a complete Night-Before Checklist combining logistics and mental preparation.

blocks.question: Retrieval from Module 4: The Off-Day Protocol starts with naming the feeling as:
blocks.explanation: Correct. 'This is mental fatigue, not laziness.' The reframe matters — the character judgment adds guilt, which adds cognitive load, which makes the adenosine problem worse.

blocks.content: The night before is the first phase of competition. Preparation replaces anxiety. When you have a structured, practiced routine, you don't have to think about what to do. Anxiety thrives in uncertainty. Structure kills uncertainty.

items.title: Mah et al. (2011) — Sleep Extension
items.content: A Stanford study on basketball players showed that extending sleep from 7.5 to 10+ hours improved sprint speed, reaction time (by a third to half a second), and shooting accuracy by 9%. Sleep isn't just recovery — it's a performance lever.

items.title: Kolling (2019) — Athletes Sleep Terribly Before Events
items.content: A review of night-before sleep disturbances confirmed what you already know: athletes sleep terribly before events. Almost universally. The anxiety, the unfamiliar environment, the early alarm — it all conspires against you. Critically, the WORRY about not sleeping was often more damaging than the actual sleep loss.

items.title: Sleep Banking
items.content: One bad night doesn't wreck performance if you've been sleeping well all week. The answer isn't perfect night-before sleep — it's banking sleep in the week leading up. The research is clear: cumulative sleep matters more than any single night.

items.title: Pre-Sleep Relaxation — The Most Effective Strategy
items.content: Kolling's review identified pre-sleep relaxation routines as the most effective strategy for improving night-before sleep quality. Athletes who reframed nerves as readiness signals — 'my body is getting ready' — and used structured relaxation outperformed those who catastrophized about poor sleep (Hanton, Mellalieu, & Hall, 2004).

items.label: Equipment Finalized by 7 PM
items.detail: Lay out everything — kit, shoes, nutrition, hydration, number, pins. All of it, physically arranged where you'll grab it in the morning. When it's laid out and visible, your brain can stop running the 'did I forget something' loop.

items.label: Worry Dump
items.detail: Open a notebook. Write down every fear, every 'what if' bouncing around your skull. Don't filter or solve — just dump. You'll discover there are 5-15 worries, not the infinite list the brain pretends. Then close the notebook. Externalizing worries is a cognitive offloading technique — your brain treats written-down concerns as 'handled.'

items.label: Highlight Reel Visualization
items.detail: Lie down. Run your Personal Highlight Reel from Module 3. The empowering memory, the crucial future moment of tomorrow's race. First-person, full sensory detail. This is the last thing your brain processes before sleep — and the sleeping brain consolidates visualized experiences overnight.

items.label: Review Your 5 If-Then Plans
items.detail: Pull out your If-Then Plans from Module 3. Read each one: 'If X happens, I will do Y.' This addresses the worries you just dumped — most already have a pre-scripted response — and primes your brain to execute those responses automatically tomorrow.

items.label: 6-2-7 Before Lights Out
items.detail: Transition directly into 6-2-7 breathing. Inhale 6, hold 2, exhale 7. Don't count cycles — just keep breathing until you drift off. The slow breathing activates the parasympathetic brake, and the counting gives the chimp busy work so it can't restart the worry loop.

blocks.content: Coach's Note: Stop checking the weather. It hasn't changed in 90 seconds.
Commitment: I will use this checklist the night before my next race on [date].
Structure kills anxiety. Have the routine. Trust the routine.
Next — the scripted sequence for race morning.
